coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots using aminoquinoline as nitrogen source as full ultraviolet bands absorber and application of LED UV leakage protection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yulin
Zhang, Bohua
Xu, Zhiqiang
Ye, Weihao
Guo, Baoyan
Zhuang, Jianle
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
Hu, Guangqi
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: LED has the advantages of small size, low power consumption, instant lighting, etc., but there is a risk of ultraviolet leakage. Long-term exposure to ultraviolet rays can lead to skin pigmentation, photoaging and additional hazards. Carbon dots (CDs) are excellent material for UV-light absorber because of their multi-functional groups and extreme stability. In this paper, A-CDs which have wide absorption range from 200 to 500 nm were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal carbonization. Then, 3&4A-CDs is prepared in two ways, CDs mixing and raw material mixing, which with average and efficient absorption efficiency both in UVA bands and UVB bands. After 180 h of accelerated aging and switched the pH value, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs still remained above 97% and 99%, and the emission wavelength did not alter. Finally, 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were synthesized and the 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film could reach 100% absorption in the full UV bond. After 180 h of accelerated aging experiments, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs@CMC film still remained above 95%. Due to the extremely excellent ultraviolet absorption efficiency of the 3&4A-CDs, the 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film can effectively avoid the leakage of ultraviolet light without affecting the visible light emitting effect of LED. At present, the synthetic process of 3&4A-CDs is simple, low cost and environmentally friendly.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
LED ultraviolet leakage protection
Mixing CDs
Full UV bands absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of 3&4A-CDs@CMC films and coating on 395 nm UV-LED chips
Application of 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating for UV leakage prevention

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) UV–vis absorption spectra and transmittance diagram of 2A-CDs to 8A-CDs. (c) Transmittance intensity diagram in UVA, UVB and visible light bands of 2A-CDs to 8A-CDs.
(a–b) TEM and HRTEM images of 3&4A-CDs. (c–e) XRD, Raman and FT-IR spectra of 3&4A-CDs. (f–i) XPS survey, C 1s, O 1s and N 1s spectra.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of 3&4A-CDs in CDs mixing and (b) in raw material mixing. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of 3&4A-CDs with different pH value in solution, (e) reaction temperature and (f) reaction time. (d) Transmittance intensity diagram in UVA, UVB, UVA + UVB and visible light bands of 3&4A-CDs with different pH value. (g) Schematic diagram of the synthesis mechanism of 3&4A-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of 3&4A-CDs. (b–c) PLE and PL spectra of 3&4A-CDs with different pH value in solution. (d) Emission center and fluorescent intensity line chart of 3&4A-CDs with different pH value in solution.

(a–b) UV–vis absorption and transmission spectra of 3&4A-CDs at different aging times. (c) acid and alkaline resistance of UV–vis absorption center and transmittance and (d) excitation center and emission center of 3&4A-CDs.
(a) Images of 3&4A-CDs@CMC films doping different concentration of 3&4A-CDs. (b) Image of the transparency and (c) UV absorption ability of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC film.
(a–b) UV–vis absorption and transmission curve of 3&4A-CDs@CMC Ⅰ films and (c–d) 3&4A-CDs@CMCⅤ films doped with different concentrations of 3&4A-CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of the UV irradiation meter usage. (b) Variation of intensity and absorption efficiency under the daylight and (c) UV-light with increasing concentration of 3&4A-CDs. (d) Intensity and absorption efficiency of 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC films at different aging time.
(a–b) UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC Ⅰ and 3&4A-CDs@CMCⅤ films at different aging times. (c) Raman and (d) XRD spectra of pure CMCⅠ and 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMCⅠ.
(a) The schematic diagram of 395 nm UV-LED chip packaging. (b–c) Spectra and color coordinates of lamp beads covered with 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating lampshades with different concentration of 3&4A-CDs.
The corresponding yield, integral area of transmittance curve and transmittance of 2A-CDs ∼ 8A-CDs in UVB bands, UVA bands, UVA + UVB bands and visible light bands.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots using aminoquinoline as nitrogen source as full ultraviolet bands absorber and application of LED UV leakage protection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education
Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou, 510665, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: LED has the advantages of small size, low power consumption, instant lighting, etc., but there is a risk of ultraviolet leakage. Long-term exposure to ultraviolet rays can lead to skin pigmentation, photoaging and additional hazards. Carbon dots (CDs) are excellent material for UV-light absorber because of their multi-functional groups and extreme stability. In this paper, A-CDs which have wide absorption range from 200 to 500 nm were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal carbonization. Then, 3&4A-CDs is prepared in two ways, CDs mixing and raw material mixing, which with average and efficient absorption efficiency both in UVA bands and UVB bands. After 180 h of accelerated aging and switched the pH value, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs still remained above 97% and 99%, and the emission wavelength did not alter. Finally, 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were synthesized and the 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film could reach 100% absorption in the full UV bond. After 180 h of accelerated aging experiments, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs@CMC film still remained above 95%. Due to the extremely excellent ultraviolet absorption efficiency of the 3&4A-CDs, the 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film can effectively avoid the leakage of ultraviolet light without affecting the visible light emitting effect of LED. At present, the synthetic process of 3&4A-CDs is simple, low cost and environmentally friendly.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet radiation, whether from the sun or man-made sources, is a significant contributor to human cancer. Among them, skin cancer and cataracts are the most common UV-related diseases [1]. The right amount of ultraviolet radiation is beneficial to health, such as killing pathogenic viruses and germs, promoting the synthesis of vitamin D in the human body, and effectively preventing rickets. However, excessive exposure or overly low protection of ultraviolet radiation will cause harm to the human body, especially in a short period of time, a large amount of exposure to ultraviolet light, may lead to skin burns, skin aging, photoallergic reactions, etc. [2]. Ultraviolet rays include UVC (200–280 nm), UVB (280–320 nm), and UVA (320–400 nm) [3]. Among them, UVB and UVA are the most critical part of LED leakage, although the intensity of their radiation is greatly lower than visible light and infrared radiation, but its photon energy exceeds the energy of C–C bond, when ultraviolet radiation combined with oxygen in the air, can make the polymer material produce free radicals, in the absence of protection, Prolonged-term exposure of human skin to UV light can lead to a variety of harmful effects. Among them, UVB can cause chronic effects such as erythema, skin burn, skin photoaging and immunosuppression, and can cause direct photochemical damage to human DNA, causing gene mutations, which is one of the main factors in the development of skin cancer. UVA can induce skin darkening, long-term exposure can cause skin damage, make the skin lose elasticity, wrinkle and induce phototoxicity and photoallergic reactions. Long-term exposure to ultraviolet light may lead to red, watery eyes and blindness, and long-term exposure to ultraviolet radiation can lead to cataracts and even blindness. Therefore, it is recommended to take sun protection measures when the UV index is 3 or more to protect the skin and eyes.

LED ultraviolet leakage refers to the ultraviolet leakage caused by various reasons during the use of LED lamps. Long-term exposure to ultraviolet rays can lead to skin pigmentation, eye damage [4], photoaging and additional hazards. However, LED has the advantages of small size, low power consumption, and instant lighting [5]. The ultraviolet light is enclosed in a narrow space, so as to prevent and control the ultraviolet light leakage and achieve human-machine coexistence, which has obvious advantages in practical applications [6]. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare an anti-ultraviolet leakage device on the LED. The UV protection design of LED lamp beads is mainly based on two aspects: material selection and process treatment. First of all, the LED lamp bead material should have high transmittance, which means that they can effectively pass through and protect visible light, and will not change the original visual effect of the image. Secondly, the surface of the material needs special treatment, such as plating a film with ultraviolet absorption ability to prevent the leakage and damage of ultraviolet rays [7]. In addition, the UV-resistant material should also have anti-aging properties to ensure that it can protect the high-power light-emitting LED lamp beads for a long time [8].

CDs are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with particle size less than 10 nm, and have many advantages such as simple preparation [9], optical [10] and chemical stability, luminescence and photobleaching [11], excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [12], environmental friendliness [13]. CDs have wide applications such as light emitting diode (LED), energy storage [14], ions detection [15], photocatalysis [16]. Since size limiting effect and photoluminescence properties, CDs can emit visible light through absorption of ultraviolet light, so it has an excellent UV absorption property to be UV absorbers [17]. However, although there have been studies on the use of CDs as ultraviolet absorbers, there are shortcomings. For example, Xu et al. have prepared N-doping CDs from waste acorn cups and added it to the polyvinyl alcohol films [18]. However, its absorption range does not cover the full ultraviolet bands. Qiu et al. prepared N-doping CDs which have full UV bands absorption [19], but it is extremely inefficient at UV absorption and visible luminescent. Xu et al. prepared a CDs using 3-aminoquinoline as a nitrogen source which is efficient at UV absorption [20], but it needs to increase the concentration to make-up for the deficiency of the UVB bands compared to the UVA bands, resulting in waste. Therefore, we have focused on modulating a CDs as a UV absorber that can achieve full UV bands absorption with excellent absorption efficiency, aging resistance and acid and alkali resistant.

In this paper, A-CDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal carbonization using anhydrous citric acid (CA) as carbon source and aminoquinoline as nitrogen source. There are multifarious absorption peaks between 301 and 408 nm and absorption range from 200 to 500 nm. After selection and experiments, 3&4A-CDs is prepared in two ways, CDs mixing and raw material mixing, which with average and efficient absorption efficiency both in UVA bands and UVB bands. After 180 h of accelerated aging experiments, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs still remained above 97%. Switched the pH value of the 3&4A-CDs aqueous solution from 1 to 12 and then back to 1 several times showed that the ultraviolet absorption intensity remained above 99% and the emission wavelength did not shift, proved the excellent stability of the 3&4A-CDs. Due to 3&4A-CDs excellent UV absorption strength stability, ultra-high UV absorption efficiency, decent solubility in the organic matrix, low impact on visible light and yellowing phenomenon, it is highly suitable as a UV absorber added to the organic matrix, so as to prepare a film with UV absorption properties. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a non-toxic organic material with high water solubility and viscosity that can be used in a variety of applications. Then, 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were synthesized and evaluated that 3&4A-CDs not only are well dissolved in CMC matrix, but also endowed them with excellent full UV bands absorption performance. The 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film could reach 100% absorption in the full UV bond and after 180 h of accelerated aging experiments, ultraviolet absorption intensity still remained above 95%. Finally, due to the excellent characteristics of 3&4A-CDs@CMC films, it is very suitable for acting as a coating for UV leakage prevention in LED. The 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film can effectively avoid the leakage of ultraviolet light without affecting the visible light emitting effect of LED, and the color coordinates of the lamp beads only shift from (0.3214,0.3223) to (0.3394,0.3389) as the concentration of 3&4A-CDs increases. At present, the synthetic process of 3&4A-CDs is simple, low cost and environmentally friendly, efficient and stable full UV absorption performance given it the potential to become a new generation of UV absorber.

2-aminoquinoline (AR), 3-aminoquinoline (AR), 4- aminoquinoline (AR), 5-aminoquinoline (AR), 6-aminoquinoline (AR), 7-aminoquinoline (AR), 8-aminoquinoline (AR), Anhydrous citric acid (AR), Anhydrous ethanol (AR), Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), Potassium hydroxide (95%) and concentrated sulfuric acid (98.08%) were purchased from Macklin Industries, Inc. All reagents without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in the whole experiment.

The visible luminescent curve was recorded by fluorescence spectrofluorometer (F-7000, Hitachi). UV–vis absorption and transmission curve were recorded by UV–vis spectrofluorometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded by Nicolet IS10360 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by SA-Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) graphics were recorded by JEOL-2010 electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns test of CDs were recorded by Ultima IV (Rigaku). The UV lamp is 365 nm and 85 W produced by Zoomlion UV Optical Factory. The Raman spectrum was recorded by Alpha300R (Germany, Wetzlar). The UV intensity in application of 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were recorded by a UV irradiometer (LS125, Linshang).

First, 0.01 M CA and 0.03 M aminoquinoline were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water, ultrasound 10 min until dissolved completely. Next, transfer it into a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave equipped with PTFE liner and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. When the solution was transformed from colorless into yellow, indicating the successful synthesis of A-CDs. The A-CDs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles. Then, a dialysis bag with molecular weight of 500 Da was used to dialyze for 36 h to remove incomplete raw materials and intermediates. Finally, the purified product was suspended in oven at 80 °C for 72 h to obtain a yellow solid CDs and calculate the yield.

First, the 3A-CDs and 4A-CDs that have been completely purified in the previous step are mixed in different proportions (1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1 and 6:1), then brought to the same concentration as A-CDs is by adding deionized water, thoroughly stirred until uniform. Then, referred to the experimental results of the finished mixed CDs, and different proportions of 3-aminoquinoline, 4-aminoquinoline and CA (2:1:3, 3:1:4, 4:1:5, 5:1:6, 6:1:7 and 7:1:8) were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water, ultrasound 10 min until dissolved completely. Next, transfer it into a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave equipped with PTFE liner and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. When the solution was transformed from colorless into yellow, indicating the successful synthesis of A-CDs. The A-CDs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles. Then, a dialysis bag with molecular weight of 500 Da was used to dialyze for 36 h to remove incomplete raw materials and intermediates. Finally, the purified product was suspended in oven at 80 °C for 72 h to obtain a yellow solid CDs and calculate the yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 3&4A-CDs@CMC films and coating on 395 nm UV-LED chips
ce:section.ce:para: Under the condition of continuous stirring at 80 °C, 2 g of carboxymethyl cellulose granules (CMCⅠ and CMCⅤ) was dissolved in 200 mL of deionized water. Then, 3&4A-CDs were weighed (0 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.3 wt%, 0.35 wt%, 0.4 wt% and 0.45 wt%) and dispersed in the pure CMC mucilage. Then, poured it to the glass slide after mixed well and dried it in the oven at 60 °C until completely to obtain CMCⅠ-0 to CMCⅠ-6 films and CMCⅤ-0 to CMCⅤ-6 films. Finally, the undried CMCⅠ-0 to CMCⅠ-6 solution were covered on the LED which has ultraviolet light leakage, and then follow-up tests.

In Fig. 1 a–b, we can find A-CDs have multifarious absorption peaks between 301 nm and 408 nm and absorption range from 200 nm to 500 nm. The absorption peak at 260–270 nm is generally attributed to the π-π* leap of the CC bond, which originates from the conjugated π orbital of the carbon ring [21]. The absorption peak around 320 nm and 365 nm belongs to the n-π* edge leap of the CO bond or CN bond and is the surface absorption state of the A-CDs [22]. Aminoquinoline is doped with N atom containing n electron in the aromatic hydrocarbon system, which is a heterocyclic compound. Compared with the benzene ring as raw material, the absorption bands of A-CDs are red-shifted and strengthened [23]. We can intuitively observe the UV absorption characteristics of each A-CDs in Fig. S1, and summarized in Fig. 1c. From the absorption peak trend of A-CDs, the absorption peak of 5A-CDs is the most redshifted. Through the molecular structure of 5-aminoquinoline, we can find that the amino group located on the benzene ring happens to be located at the farthest end of the N in the heterocyclic ring [24]. On the one hand, due to the steric effect, the farther the amino group is from the N in the heterocyclic ring, the more conducive to the dehydration condensation reaction during the formation of A-CDs [25]. On the other hand, because the amino group is located on the benzene ring, the benzene ring is more involved in the formation of carbon points, and the CDs structure is more compact [26]. According to the molecular orbital theory, the CC and benzene rings can increase the π electron groups, resulting in π-π* transition, and the conjugation effect makes the π electrons further delocalized and flow in the entire conjugated system [27]. This delocalization effect makes the orbitals have greater bonding properties, thereby reducing the energy, making the π electrons easy to excite, and the absorption peak redshift [28]. This is also why the absorption peak of 6A-CDs to 8A-CDs is more redshifted than that of 2A-CDs to 4A-CDs.

From the ultraviolet absorption and transmission diagram and Table 1 , we can find that 3A-CDs and 4A-CDs have excellent absorption intensity in the UVA and UVB bands, respectively. At this time, we can mix the two to obtain a CDs with average and high efficiency absorption intensity in both UVA and UVB bands. The purpose of this is that 3-aminoquinoline and 4-aminoquinoline are close in chemical structure and properties, and can be more conducive to the synthesis of CDs than mixing with other raw materials.

The TEM photographs in Fig. 2 a confirmed that 3&4A-CDs have excellent dispersion in aqueous solution with an average particle size about 2.76 nm. In Fig. 2b, the HRTEM images showed a 0.21 nm lattice stripes, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite [29]. The XRD pattern of the 3&4A-CDs in Fig. 2c has a characteristic peak at 2θ = 22°, which corresponding to Ref. [1] the graphite (100) plane [30]. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 2d shows two characteristic absorption bands at 1367.028 cm-1 (D-band) and 1569.517 cm-1 (G-band), respectively. The characteristic absorption band at 1367.028 cm-1 (D-band) indicates the existence of C–C of sp3 character and the characteristic absorption band at 1569.517 cm-1 (G-band) indicates the existence of CC of sp2 character, and ID/IG = 0.94 suggested a high degree of carbonization [20]. The result shows that 3&4A-CDs a has a certain degree of carbonization.

In Fig. 2e, the chemical composition and structure of 3&4A-CDs were further investigated. There is a broad absorption band at 3448 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of the N–H and –OH bonds [31], the absorption peak at 2989 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the -C-H [32]. The absorption peak at 1618 cm−1 and 1391 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC [33], respectively. According to the above discussion, the surface of 3&4A-CDs contains abundant functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2. The XPS full-scan spectra of the 3&4A-CDs and the high-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s are listed in Fig. 2f-i. The XPS full-scan spectra in Fig. 2f show that 3&4A-CDs are mainly composed of C, O and N elements with binding energies of 285.18 eV, 400.12 eV and 533.81 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [34]. The percentages of C, N and O atoms are 76.34%, 8.12% and 15.54%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s shows the presence of CC/C–C (284.84 eV), C–N (285.88 eV), C–O (286.41 eV) and CO/CN (288.59 eV) [35]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s shows the presence of CO(531.91 eV) and C–O(533.42 eV) in Fig. 2h [36]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s shows three peaks of N–H (398.88 eV), pyridinic N (399.38 eV), and graphitic N (401.58 eV) in Fig. 2i [37]. The results of XPS and FT-IR indicate that 3&4A-CDs contain –OH, CO, –NH2, CN and other functional groups on the surface.

3&4A-CDs have excellent absorption in the full UV bands. From Fig. 3 a, we can see that as the ratio of 3A-CDs:4A-CDs gradually increases, the UV absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs obtained from CDs mixing continues to increase, and when the ratio is 6:1, the intensity of UVA and UVB bands reaches an average. With reference to the above results, we proceeded to prepare 3&4A-CDs by mixing raw materials. As 3A-CDs and 4A-CDs participate in the formation of 3&4A-CDs as nitrogen sources at the same time, the 3&4A-CDs products are more pure, and there is no difference in chemical structure between different CDs, which is difficult to control their respective properties under different conditions; The first part of hydrothermal reaction is the dehydration condensation reaction between –OH, –H and –COOH between CA molecules [38], with further polymerization, carbonization and aromatization, sp2 carbon clusters were formed containing a large number of –COOH, –OH on the surface [39]. 3-aminoquinoline and 4-aminoquinoline provide a basic condition for hydrothermal carbonization, and the amino group of aminoquinoline will undergo dehydration condensation reaction with CA, and finally form the crosslinked chain connected by amide group. As 3-aminoquinoline and 4-aminoquinoline began to participate in the reaction, the corresponding absorption peak at 320 nm and 370 nm began to increase, and with the increase of the addition of 3-aminoquinoline, the absorption peak at 370 nm enhanced shown in Fig. 3b, and when the ratio of 3-aminoquinoline:4-aminoquinoline:CA is 5:1:6, the intensity of UVA and UVB bands reaches an average (see Fig. 4).

The abundant functional groups on 3&4A-CDs surface cause its ultraviolet absorption intensity to be considerably affected by pH value shown in Fig. 3c–d, that strong acids tend to enhance the ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs and redshift the absorption peak. On the one hand, due to conjugate aromatic π-systems in alkaline, strong acids can enhance the charge transfer of n ions [40]. On the other hand, it could be because of attribution by the C–N–C bond and –NH2 contained in 3-aminoquinoline and 4-aminoquinoline [41]. Then, to explore the optimum conditions for preparing 3&4A-CDs, three reaction times (6 h, 8 h and 10 h) and three reaction temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C) are set, 8 h and 180 °C are the best shown in Fig. 2e–f, respectively. Summary, the full UV bands absorbing 3&4A-CDs were successfully synthesized, the peak of 260 nm rise from carbon nucleus, including saturated hydrocarbons containing O and N. The CC and CO bonds generated 320 nm absorption peak, and peak nearly 370 nm is from the CN bond.

Due to photoluminescent properties and size-limiting effect, CDs can emit visible light under UV excitation [42]. The 3&4A-CDs showed an excitation band at 380 nm, which stem from the major uniform size of the luminescence sp2 carbon clusters in the 3&4A-CDs. The maximum excitation and fluorescence wavelengths were 380 and 465 nm. Meanwhile, three peaks of the UV absorption curve of 3&4A-CDs highly covered the peaks of the excitation curve in 280 nm, 320 nm and 370 nm, indicating that the 3&4A-CDs can make full use of the absorbed UV light and convert it into 470 nm visible light. The fluorescence of 3&4A-CDs is same as UV absorption, which is greatly affected by pH value in Fig. 3b–d. When the pH value ranges from 1 to 6, the emission center is about 465 nm, however, when the pH value ranges from 7 to 12, the emission center blue-shifts to 420 nm, which might be due to the abundant positively charged surface functional groups [43]. Under acidic conditions, 3&4A-CDs has higher fluorescent luminous intensity may be attributed to conjugate aromatic π-systems in alkaline and attribution by the C–N–C bond and –NH2 contained in aminoquinoline [44]. Fig. S2 shows that the fluorescence of 3&4A-CDs is excitation independence, because the surface of the 3&4A-CDs is completely passivated under the best reaction conditions and raw material ratio, is only attributed to the single radiation transition of the sp2 carbon cluster [45].

In order to explore the photobleaching stability of 3&4A-CDs, an accelerated aging experiment was set in Fig. 5 a–b. After exposing 3&4A-CDs to UV lamps for 180 h, the ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs still remained above 97% and the fluorescence intensity remained above 95%. In Figs. S5 and S6, the pH value of the 3&4A-CDs changed from 1 to 12 and then back to 1 seven times showed that a reversible and stable cycle of both the ultraviolet absorption intensity and fluorescence intensity of 3&4A-CDs and remained both above 99%, and the absorption peaks and fluorescence peaks did not change, proved the excellent stability of the 3&4A-CDs. In conclusion, 3&4A-CDs can be confirmed an excellent stability and are the best choice for outdoor full-UV bands absorption material [46].

From Fig. 6 a, with the increase of 3&4A-CDs concentration, we can find that the color of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films gradually changed from colorless to light yellow, but the 3&4A-CDs@CMC film is still with elevated visible light transmittance in Fig. 6b. Fig. 6c shows that when the 3&4A-CDs solution is directly illuminated by an UV lamp, it emits bright blue light, but when we place a 3&4A-CDs@CMC film between the UV lamp and the 3&4A-CDs solution, the ultraviolet is effectively blocked by the 3&4A-CDs@CMC film and the 3&4A-CDs solution no longer emits bright blue light.

The absorption efficiency of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were proved in Fig. 7 a and c that the pure CMC film almost has no UV absorption intensity, which indicates that the absorption performance of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films is virtually due to 3&4A-CDs. Due to the different molecular weight of CMC matrix (CMCⅠ and CMCⅤ), the UV absorption peak of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films is slightly different, but we found that the transmittance of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films with only 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs concentration could reach 100% in the full UV bands both CMCⅠ and CMCⅤ. With the increasing content of 3&4A-CDs doped in CMC matrix, the wavelength of absorption red-shifted, and the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films transfer from a single UV shielding to a weak blue shielding.

Due to the particularity of LED requiring prolonged-term luminescence, 3&4A-CDs@CMC films should maintain extremely excellent photobleaching resistance as 3&4A-CDs. We conducted the same accelerated aging experiment as 3&4A-CDs, after exposing 3&4A-CDs@CMC films to UV lamps for 180 h, the ultraviolet absorption intensity had slightly weakened and still remained above 95% both 3&4A-CDs@CMC Ⅰ films and 3&4A-CDs@CMCⅤ films. It is confirmed the photobleaching resistance of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films are very excellent, and it is suitable for long-term exposure to glowing LED. Various CDs cannot dissolve when mixed with organic matrix, or even if dissolved, it will also change the original chemical structure and properties of the matrix [47]. Therefore pure CMC films and 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were tested by Raman, XRD and FT-IR in Fig. 9 c–d and S7. The result turns out that the incorporation of 3&4A-CDs into the CMC film did not change the chemical structure and properties of CMC matrix, but increased the ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs@CMC films by adding 3&4A-CDs. In summary, the above experimental results prove that 3&4A-CDs are suitable to be added into CMC matrix as an ultraviolet absorber, and the 3&4A-CDs@CMC films is also suitable for use in Application of 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating for UV leakage prevention.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating for UV leakage prevention
ce:section.ce:para: In the UV leakage prevention in 395 nm UV-LED chips shown in Fig. 10 a, the blue, green, and red primary color phosphors are mixed in AB glue in the ratio of 1.5:2:1 and dot-mapped on a 395 nm UV-chip. Then, the 3&4A-CDs@CMC with different concentrations of 3&4A-CDs was coated on the inner surface of the lampshade, and then the lampshade was installed after the 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating has dried. The spectra of the encapsulated lamp beads were tested with spectral color illuminance meter in Fig. 10b. It can be seen that the intensity peak of the UV chip becomes lower when the concentration of 3&4A-CDs increases. When the concentration of 3&4A-CDs was 0.45 wt%, the intensity peak of the UV chip almost disappeared, and the 3&4A-CDs coating did not affect the intensity in the visible region. This result is consistent with the results of the UV–Vis transmission spectra of the 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating on the CIE coordinate system in Fig. 10c. It can be seen that the color coordinates of the lamp beads shifted from (0.3214,0.3223) to (0.3394,0.3389) as the concentration of 3&4A-CDs increases. Thus, we can prove that the 3&4A-CDs@CMC coating can effectively avoid the leakage of ultraviolet light without affecting the visible light emitting effect of LED.

In summary, A-CDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal carbonization using anhydrous CA as carbon source and aminoquinoline as nitrogen source, which have multifarious absorption peaks between 301 and 408 nm and absorption range from 200 to 500 nm. Then, 3&4A-CDs is prepared in two ways, CDs mixing and raw material mixing, which with average and efficient absorption efficiency both in UVA bands and UVB bands. After 180 h of accelerated aging and switched the pH value from 1 to 12 and then back to 1 several times, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs still remained above 97% and 99%, and the emission wavelength did not change. Finally, 3&4A-CDs@CMC films were synthesized and the 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film could reach 100% absorption in the full UV bond. After 180 h of accelerated aging experiments, ultraviolet absorption intensity of 3&4A-CDs@CMC film still remained above 95%. Due to the extremely excellent ultraviolet absorption efficiency of the 3&4A-CDs, the 0.45 wt% 3&4A-CDs@CMC film can effectively avoid the leakage of ultraviolet light without affecting the visible light emitting effect of LED, and the color coordinates of the lamp beads only change from (0.3214,0.3223) to (0.3394,0.3389) as the concentration of 3&4A-CDs increases. This study makes aminoquinoline give full play to its potential as nitrogen source to prepare CDs as ultraviolet absorber, and provides an excellent solution for LED anti-ultraviolet leakage.

Yulin Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Bohua Zhang: Conceptualization. Zhiqiang Xu: Data curation. Weihao Ye: Formal analysis. Baoyan Guo: Conceptualization. Jianle Zhuang: Conceptualization. Chaofan Hu: Conceptualization. Bingfu Lei: Conceptualization. Guangqi Hu: Conceptualization. Yingliang Liu: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.112060.
